If backlog conversion slips further or if margins fail to stabilize by the second half of fiscal 2025, SLP’s premium valuation, already trimmed down to that of 27.89x P/E, with consensus expecting a further compression by 2027, could come under sharper pressure.

Final Rating: Hold

I’m sticking with my Hold rating for SLP. The strength in their software growth and the promising bits in the pipeline are encouraging enough, but then I look at the persistent margin erosion, the cash flow pressures, and the sluggish service execution, and my enthusiasm starts to fade. What I’d really like, before upgrading to a "Buy", is some clearer proof that they can actually sort out their operations and turn that backlog into something useful. For the time being, the risk and reward seem to be cancelling each other out nicely, and I’d much rather wait to see if management can get profitability back on its feet and keep growth from wandering off.

data.title: Why A Barbell Income Portfolio Makes Sense Today
data.description:
A barbell portfolio is one of the many ways to manage an investment allocation. In this article, we take a look at this allocation type in the context of income investing. In our view, barbell portfolios have a lot to recommend them. Although they don't typically sport the highest yields, they carry a number of benefits that can allow them to outperform over the longer term. Below we explore their features and benefits, how they can apply to income investing and what types of securities can work in their construction.

What Is A Barbell Portfolio?

There are many different types of barbell portfolios. Conceptually, the idea of a barbell portfolio is that rather than holding everything along the asset spectrum, an investor instead picks two different (i.e., perhaps polar opposite, hence the barbell name) types of assets in the portfolio. The idea is that because the two assets will tend to behave differently in various market scenarios, their combination provides both participation in the market as well as diversification.

In the context of income investing, "quality" tends to be the key differentiator, with a common barbell composed of higher quality / lower-yielding and lower-quality / higher-yielding assets. However, in our view, quality is not expansive enough a criterion. It can also mislead investors into thinking that, say, a portfolio composed of CLO Equity CEFs and Muni CEFs is adequate because it spans the gamut of credit quality.

In our view, rather than quality, investors should think about "risk" as the key differentiator between the two barbell assets. "Quality" tends to stand in for sector (i.e., junk bonds are lower-quality; investment-grade bonds are higher-quality), and therefore, it ignores many other factors that make a barbell portfolio work.

Specifically, we would focus on four different risk factors to vary across the income barbell – sector, leverage, duration and discounts. Both of these factors have an impact on both yield and risk that a given asset produces.

Taking sector first, higher-risk sectors include loans and high-yield corporate bonds, while lower-risk sectors include munis and preferreds. By sector risk we primarily mean market volatility and likelihood of permanent economic losses.

Duration is rarely viewed as a standalone risk factor, however it is nonetheless a key risk consideration. Many investors found out in 2022 that many "high-quality" assets such as municipal bonds delivered sharp losses to investors which are, if not permanent, likely long-term in nature.

Leverage is straight-forward – it magnifies the risk of a given asset, i.e., a leveraged loan CEF is higher-risk than an unleveraged loan CEF, all else equal. Whether leverage produces additional yield is not a given – in an environment of a flat yield curve and tight spreads, leverage generates little additional yield for most credit CEFs.

On the discount side – many investors know it in their bones that discount behavior is procyclical, i.e., they exacerbate up and down moves, so if the NAV drop is 5%, the price drop might be 7% due to additional discount widening and vice versa.

What this breakdown means is that on the lower-risk side of the barbell it’s a good idea to target 1) higher-credit quality sectors such as munis and preferreds, 2) assets with no explicit leverage such as individual bonds and preferreds (rather than, say, leveraged preferred CEFs) and 3) securities that don’t trade at discounts to NAV such as open-end funds, bonds and preferreds.

Duration is more complicated. Longer-duration / higher-credit quality assets such as munis and some preferreds can protect investors in a macro downturn when longer-term rates drop and credit spreads widen. However, they may not protect investors from an inflation shock, as many found out in 2022. For this reason, it's a good idea to have a balanced mix of longer-duration and shorter-duration assets. In the latter bucket, we would include floating-rate assets and bonds with a maturity of 2–3 years.

Can a barbell be built solely out of CEFs? It can be, however, it would miss out on the benefits of a full barbell portfolio so much that it would barely qualify as a barbell. Recall that a barbell requires not just assets from different sectors but with different risk characteristics to be optimized.

It's also important to highlight that there is a trade-off in a barbell portfolio. That trade-off has to do with yield on one hand and diversification / potential rotation / lower volatility on the other. A portfolio that is "all in" will tend to have a higher yield than a barbell portfolio since a barbell portfolio will, almost by definition, have assets with a lower yield.

A barbell portfolio, on the other hand, will tend to exhibit less volatility due to the lower-risk bucket. This will not only keep behavioral biases at bay (i.e., for example, the tendency to sell at the lows and buy back higher due to excessive portfolio drawdowns) but will also allow investors to take advantage of the drawdown by rotating from lower-risk assets to higher-yielding assets.

This last factor is an essential feature and a key benefit of the barbell. A barbell portfolio provides positive convexity by allowing the investor to rotate between the two barbell portions. The investor, in effect, sells high and buys low over time by having the freedom to move capital into the lower-risk portion when valuations are expensive and vice versa. This creates another source of return, allowing the investor to monetize high volatility.

Barbell Ideas

On the higher-risk / yield side of the barbell, we like a number of floating-rate assets, such as BDCs and CLO Equity securities. The sectors behind these assets can be very volatile, and they are exposed to credit losses in a recession. However, they also deliver high yields and allow investors to participate in strong market returns.

Some of our BDC holdings include:

  • Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending (NCDL), trading at a 10.3% yield and a 4% discount,
  • Bain Capital Specialty Finance (BCSF), trading 10.7% yield and a 5% discount,
  • Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), trading at a 10.2% yield and a 2% premium

On the CLO Equity side, we like:

  • CLO Equity / Loan CEF XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT), trading at a 15.5% yield and a 3% discount.
  • CLO Equity CEF Pearl Diver Credit Co (PDCC), trading at an 18.8% yield and a 4% discount

On the other side of the barbell we like a mix of open-end funds, individual preferreds and bonds.

  • Agency mortgage REIT Dynex Capital Series C (DX.PR.C), which will shortly shift to a near 10% yield when it floats in mid-April. The stock has the highest level of equity coverage in the sector and offers very strong risk/reward,
  • Municipal mutual fund First Eagle High Yield Municipal Fund (FEHAX) with a 4.9% distribution rate.
  • Baby bond from CLO Equity CEF OXLC (OXLCI), trading at an 8.2% yield.

Takeaways

A barbell portfolio has a number of advantages for income investors.

First, it provides quality diversification from the top down. A bottom-up approach to portfolio construction can often get away from investors, resulting in a portfolio that does not actually reflect the investor's overall risk appetite.

Secondly, it creates the potential for rebalancing and using the lower-risk portion of the portfolio as a source of "dry powder" to take advantage of attractive higher-yielding opportunities during market drawdowns.

Finally, a barbell approach mitigates the worst-case scenario outcome due to the presence of higher-quality assets in the portfolio.

Over 2024 we have mostly been reducing our allocation in the higher-risk portion of the barbell, most notably by reducing our CEF sleeve and rotating into individual preferreds and bonds. Tight credit spreads and discounts, as well as modest additional income on leveraged assets meant that the opportunity cost for being in higher-risk assets was minimal.

The recent market environment has been favorable for a barbell allocation approach. Risk sentiment has soured, which has increased volatility and dispersion in the market, opening up new opportunities. Credit spreads have also widened, which has pushed down the valuation of some of the more volatile sectors such as CLO Equity and opened up discounts in some CEFs and BDCs. We have taken advantage of this dynamic and have topped up the CEF/BDC portion of the barbell in the last month or two.
